titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Hanson Weightman Baldwin Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday July 29, 2020 at 07:15
Finding aid revision description not supplied.

did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, writings, subject files, research materials, publicity for books, and other papers of Hanson W. Baldwin, Pulitzer Prize winning journalist and military affairs reporter and editor for the New York Times from 1929-1968, and editor for Reader's Digest , 1968-1976. The papers relate to Baldwin's work and interests as a journalist and author and include correspondence with many high-ranking officers of the armed services, government officials, and writers and historians, as well as other members of the staff of the New York Times and Reader's Digest . Of particular interest are the subject files of printed materials and clippings which Baldwin collected and maintained for his own use. Included in these files are a number of important reports, transcriptions, and other items, some of which are not easily obtainable elsewhere.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

prefercite.head: Cite As
prefercite.p: Hanson Weightman Baldwin Papers (MS 54). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition note
acqinfo.p: Gift of Hanson Baldwin in 1968, 1978, 1988, and 1989. Gift of Barbara Potter, 1994.

scopecontent.head: Description of the Papers
scopecontent.p: The Hanson Weightman Baldwin Papers consists of materials relating to Baldwin's work and interests as journalist and author. They include correspondence, writings by Baldwin in draft and published form, and printed matter of various types on military and political topics collected by Baldwin.
The Hanson Baldwin Papers are divided into five series: CORRESPONDENCE, WRITINGS, SUBJECT FILE, PRINTED MATTER and SPECIAL FILES.
CORRESPONDENCE is divided into three sections: "General Correspondence," "Correspondence on Writings," and "Correspondence of Others."

"General Correspondence" consists of letters to Baldwin or his assistant (George Barrett and later Edward Mossien) and his own retained file copies of replies. Baldwin corresponded with numerous high-ranking military personnel, government officials, and other writers and historians. Some letters are of a purely business nature, arranging lectures and visits or requesting and sending official information, but many do discuss military and political affairs. There are almost no letters of a purely personal nature or which do not in some way relate to Baldwin's work. Correspondents of note include among others: Arleigh A. Burke, Louis Denfeld, Allen W. Dulles, Ferdinand Eberstadt, E.M. Eller, Basil H. Liddell Hart, Roger Hilsman, Alfred A. Knopf, Frank Knox, George C. Marshall, Louis Morton, Richard Nixon, William E. Potter, William J. Sebald, Robert Sherrod, Harold R. Stark, Carl Vinson (and other members of the House Committee on Armed Services) and Gerauld Wright. There is also a section of correspondence between Baldwin and other members of the New York Times staff, including Julius Ochs Adler, Arthur Hays Sulzberger, and Arthur Ochs Sulzberger. In addition, there is a large section of correspondence with various U.S. government departments (mainly the Department of Defense.) Correspondence with an individual who held several different offical positions in the course of his career, has been grouped together under the person's name, and a cross-reference made for each position.

"Correspondence on Writings" consists of correspondence between Baldwin and others about his books and articles. This section is not complete, however, and correspondence about addresses and other writings may be found in "General Correspondence." (Cross references have been made where there is known to be correspondence about the article or book in question in "General Correspondence".) Most of the correspondence in this section consists of reactions to Baldwin's writings from members of the public, or of business correspondence with publishers. There is a large section of correspondence with other writers concerning the book series edited by Baldwin, Great Battles of History , but this also is mainly of a business or editorial nature. Correspondents of note in this section include William F. Halsey and Thomas C. Kinkaid, who contributed notes to Baldwin's chapter on "The Battle for Leyte Gulf" in Seafights and Shipwrecks . (Kinkaid's notes are filed with the book manuscript in Series II.) There is also a letter from the Hill-stead Museum enclosing a copy of an account by one of the survivors of the Lusitania, and a section of letters referring to Baldwin's T.V. appearance on C.B.S.'s 1965 program, "Hawks and Doves."

"Correspondence of others" consists of correspondence between persons other than Baldwin. Much of this correspondence is in the form of copies, which were probably sent to Baldwin for reference by one of the correspondents. A large section consists of letters to other members of the New York Times staff. Of particular interest are copies of letters from John R. McKone and Bruce F. Olmstead to their wives, written while the two airmen were prisoners in Russia in 1960.

Series II, WRITINGS, consists of manuscript material for and published editions of addresses, articles, books written or edited by Baldwin, together with a small amount of research and publicity material. There are also some accounts of his radio and television appearances. Articles have been divided into those identifiable as having been written for the New York Times , and articles written for other publications. At the end of the section of the New York Times articles is a small section of inter-office memos concerning editorial cuts and changes which had been made in Baldwin's articles. In most cases these memos are attached to the manuscript or printed version of the article in question. There is a small section of writings by others on which Baldwin has written notes and comments.

Series III, SUBJECT FILE, consists of Baldwin's own reference files on military and political subjects. They contain clippings of his own articles and other newspaper clippings, notes by himself and others, and printed matter of various types. The allocation of material to any particular subject heading is largely Baldwin's own. Correspondence originally kept in these files has been moved to CORRESPONDENCE in order to consolidate letters from any one individual, but a list of these has been left under the relevant subject heading. There is inevitably some overlap of information filed under different subject headings, but cross-references have been made where possible. Additional information on many of the subjects will be found in Series IV, PRINTED MATTER.
The series is divided into four main sections:

U.S. Military Information is divided into five sub-sections: "U.S. Defense", U.S. Air Force", "U.S. Army", "U.S. Navy" and "U.S. Territorial Possessions". "U.S. Defense" contains material relating to general defense policies and topics, and material relating to more than one of the armed services. Of interest are materials on the B36 and the TFX, filed under 'Aviation', and Baldwin's memoranda on the F.B.I. investigation of him filed under 'Loyalty, Secrecy and Security'. The next three sub-sections contain material relating to the Air Force, the Army and the Navy respectively. "U.S. Territorial Possessions" consists of material relating to Alaska, Hawaii, the Panama Canal and the Virgin Islands.

Foreign Military Information consists of material relating to foreign countries and areas of the world. This often includes information on U.S. foreign policy in these areas, such as Korea and Vietnam (on which there are large quantities of material). There are also fairly large sections on Germany and Great Britain.

General Military Information consists of material which does not refer exclusively to either the United States or any one foreign country. A large portion of this material provides information on both the U.S. and the U.S.S.R., including materialon missiles and on nuclear weapons.

Wars consists of material relating principally to World War II, but occasionally to the Korean War or Vietnam as well. This has been indicated on the folders where appropriate. This section is divided into three sub-sections: "Europe and Africa", "The Pacific and Asia", and "General Topics". The first two sections contain material relating to combat in those areas of the world. "General Topics" consists of material relating to subjects relevant to all areas and phases of war, such as casualties, naval engagements, the importance of rubber, and so on.

Series IV, PRINTED MATTER, consists of government documents, U.S. service manuals, periodicals, commerical pamphlets, and reports and studies by various individuals and organizations, and has been roughly grouped under subject headings which correspond with those in Series III (SUBJECT FILE.) Of particular interest are the Council on Foreign Relations' "Studies of the American Interests in the War and the Peace"; the government hearings on the T.F.X. contract; reports and studies of the first and second Hoover Commission; and some pre-World War II Russian propaganda books written in English and published in Moscow.
Series V, SPECIAL FILES, consists of biographical material about Baldwin (mainly clippings), contracts, legal and personal documents, and notes by Baldwin.
SUBSEQUENT ACCESSIONS

This addition to the Hanson Baldwin Papers is comprised of five accessions which have been merged, totaling 16.5 linear feet. The addition is divided into five series: Correspondence (Series I), Research Material (Series 10, Writings and Related Material (Series III), Personal Material (Series IV), and Photographs (Series V). These series generally follow the five series of the main body of the papers. Series III (Subjects File) and Series IV (Printed Matter) of the main accession have been combined into Series II (Research Material) in the addition. Series V (Special Files) of the main accession has been separated into Series IV (Personal Material) and Series V (Photographs) in the addition.

Most of material in the addition dates from the 1960s and 1970s. The most thorough documentation is of the mid-1960s to the mid-1970s. There is much more material from those years in the addition than in the main accession, especially for the period following Baldwin's retirement from the New York Times in 1968. There are also documents relating to Hanson Baldwin's family, especially his father, O. P. Baldwin, Jr., and a greater number of photographs than are found in the main accession.

Baldwin's career as a journalist and author and his interactions with other journalists, military leaders, and politicians are well-documented in the addition. He traveled extensively throughout the world, and there is substantive material documenting these trips, especially to Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. The addition also contains quite interesting material on the internal workings of the New York Times and Reader's Digest.
A detailed bibliography of Baldwin's newspaper articles editorials, book reviews, and feature stories is available in the repository.

bioghist.head: Biographical History
bioghist.p: Hanson Weightman Baldwin was born in Baltimore, Maryland on March 22, 1903. His father, Oliver Perry Baldwin, was editor of the Baltimore Sun. Baldwin was educated at the Boys' Latin School, Baltimore, and graduated from the U.S. Naval Academy, Annapolis, in 1924. He was commissioned an ensign in the U.S. Navy and served on battleships and a destroyer on the East coast, in the Caribbean, and in a European squadron. In 1927 he resigned in order to travel and write. He spent a year as a quartermaster in the merchant marine and in 1928 joined the Baltimore Sun as a general assignment and police reporter.

In 1929 Baldwin joined the staff of the New York Times . Because of his background and interests the New York Times gave him an increasing number of naval and military assignments. In 1937 Baldwin spent four months in Europe covering the armaments build-up. In 1942 he was awarded the Pulitzer prize for his articles on the war in the Pacific. In the same year he became the military editor of the New York Times . Until his retirement in 1968, Baldwin travelled extensively and covered major military events for the Times all over the world. He was present at the Normandy invasion and the second atomic test at Bikini; he went to Korea, Japan and Formosa in 1950, to Berlin in 1961, and to Vietnam in 1965 and again in 1968.

In 1940 Baldwin became co-chairman with Allen Dulles of the armaments group of the Council on Foreign Relations' "Studies of American Interests in the War and the Peace." He was also a member of the Security Task Force of the first Hoover Commission and was a frequent witness before various government committees. Nevertheless, Baldwin believed that his major responsibility was to the public and the nation, and not to any administration. If he received classified information in confidence it was not revealed, but "no newspaper and no commentator," he insisted, "can regard a classified stamp as a bar to publication or virtually no newspaper could be published." (See correspondence with Albert L. Kotzebue.) In 1962 Baldwin was investigated by the F.B.I. for publishing information about Russian missile bases which was not known to some high-ranking government officials.

Baldwin wrote numerous articles for periodicals, as well as those for the New York Times ; he frequently gave talks at service schools and academies, and he appeared both on radio and television. For a list of his published writings see Series II, "Writings," and the Appendix to these papers.

In 1938 Baldwin co-edited, with Shepard Stone, We Saw It Happen , a collection of stories by New York Times journalists. Between 1960 and 1968 he edited a series of books for J.B. Lippincott called Great Battles of History .
Baldwin married Helen Bruce in 1931. They had two daughters and several grandchildren.

odd.head: APPENDIX
odd.p: The newspaper clippings containing Baldwin's articles, editorials, book reviews, and feature stories were in poor condition and could not be saved. In order to aid the researcher in locating these materials in the newspaper collections of the library, a chronological list has been prepared which includes the publisher and the title of the article, when it is known. Undated newspaper articles are located at the end of the collection (Box 140).
For additional Baldwin writings see Series II.

"Millions Here Watch Solar Eclipse, Clouds Mar Spectacle for Science at Many Points in Totality Belt"
"City in Darkness Pays Homage to Edison, Joins Nation in Moment's Silent Tribute"
"Retailers Fight Sales Tax at State Heating As Blow to Return of Prosperity"
"Grandi for Arms Act on Basis of Security"
"View New Hydrogen As Aid to Science"
"Not a Liquor Death Here on New Year's"
"Levy is Honored As Fund Drive Ends"
"Boy, Hidden By Box, Is Killed By Truck"
"Huge New Press With 'Brain' Produces 14 Complete Newspapers a Second"
"Honor to War Dead Will Be Paid Today Throughout Nation"
"Injuries on Fourth At a New Low Here"
"City and Nation Hold Safest and Sanest Independence Day"

"Life on Fire Escape Ends in Real Home"
"Vast Uptown Area to be Stake in Suit"

"Nassau Policeman Held in 3rd Degree Death, 6 More Will Be Suspended"
"Critics of Edwards Ask Governor to Act In 3rd Degree Inquiry"
"Fireman Killed, 31 Injured By Blast In Ritz Tower Fire"
"Seized In Fake Raid On Renthouse Club"
"Escaped Parrot Flutters About Times Square Until Efforts of Unsung Heroes Effect Capture"
"Convicts Slain In Riot On Welfare Island"
"1932 Is Hooted Out, New Year Greeted With Noisy Revelry"
"Dr. R. V. Johnson, 80, Not An 'Old Fogey'"
"New Cultural Bonds Link US to Ireland, Says Yeats At Dinner of Irish Notables"
"Navy Games Leave Doubt Air Raiders Could Bomb Coast"'
"Former Secretary of the Navy Commends Menoeuvers Report"
"1,800 March Here In Army Day Parade"

"Thin Ranks of GAR Cling to their Place in Memorial Parade"

"Dress Strike Peace Is Near as NBA Acts"

"Harbor Is Noisy With Salutes As British Cruiser Arrives"

"Borah Hails Rise of Nationalism, Urges Isolationism"
"New Era Opens For the Navy, With Treaty Limit the Goal"
"McKee Thinks City Is Under A Blight"

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in five series and one addition: I. Correspondence, 1931-1968. II. Writings, 1934-1968. III. Subjects File, n.d. IV. Printed Matter, 1940-1946. V. Special Files, 1931-1967. The addition is arranged in five series: I. Correspondence, 1928-88. II. Writings, 1934-68. III. Subjects File, n.d. IV. Printed Matter, 1940-1964, n.d. V. Photographs, 1910-1970s.

geogname.part: Soviet Union -- Military relations -- United States
United States -- Military relations -- Soviet Union
United States -- Politics and government -- 20th Century

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: CORRESPONDENCE is divided into three sections:

General Correspondence arranged alphabetically by the name of the person or organization with whom Baldwin corresponded. Important correspondence and the correspondence of notable persons has generally been individually foldered and filed after those materials labeled with a letter of the alphabet. Note Correspondence with all government and military agencies and departments is filed under the general heading, "UNITED STATES GOVERNMENT," and then under the name of the agency or department.
Correspondence on Writings arranged alphabetically by the type of writing, then chronologically by the date of publication (with the exception of books, which are arranged alphabetically by title).
Correspondence of Others arranged alphabetically by the name of the person or organization to whom the letter is addressed, then alphabetically by the name of the correspondent.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WRITINGS is arranged alphabetically by type, then chronologically by the date of publication (with the exception of books, which are arranged alphabetically by title). Reviews, publicity, and research materials have been filed under the appropriate title.

In order to aid the researcher in identifying and locating Baldwin's newspaper articles, editorials, book reviews, and feature stories, a list consisting of the dates of publication and the name of the newspaper in which they were published has been appended to this register. This chronological list also includes the titles for most of Baldwin's pre-World War II articles. Undated newspaper articles, which are not listed in the Appendix, are located at the end of the collection (Box 140).

did.unittitle: "The Public and the Future of the Armed Forces"

"The Place of War in International Relations"
"The Effect of Foreign Policy and Public Opinion of Military Strategy"

"The Suez Crisis as Part of the Communist-free World Struggle"
"Comparative Ability of the Free World and Soviet Bloc to Further Objectives through Military Power"
"Military Considerations in the Development of National Security Policy"
"Military Preparedness as a Factor of National Power"
discussion of U.S. foreign policy in Europe
"At the Turning Point in the Study of War"
programs and memoranda, without copies of address

unittitle.emph: Anthologies including articles and book chapters by Baldwin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: SUBJECTS FILE is divided into sections and sub-sections as follows: U.S. MILITARY INFORMATION: U.S. Defense, U.S. Air Force, U.S. Army, U.S. Navy, U.S. Territorial Possessions; FOREIGN MILITARY INFORMATION; GENERAL MILITARY INFORMATION; WARS: Europe and Africa, Pacific and Asia, General Topics.
Within each section material is arranged alphabetically by subject heading.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The correspondence series (5.5 linear feet) documents Baldwin's work as a journalist at the New York Times and Reader's Digest and as the author and editor of numerous books and articles. The series is arranged in two sections: chronological and alphabetical. The bulk of the correspondence in the chronological section (4.0 linear ft.) is from 1966 to 1975. Most of the alphabetical correspondence (1.5 linear ft.) is from 1974 to 1980. In many cases Baldwin stapled letters and his response together, often annotating envelopes with comments or a record of his response. There is some correspondence located in other series, noted in the description for those series.

The earliest items in the chronological section are letters to Hanson Baldwin from his family and friends, 1928-1931. The letters mention family events and social matters, as well as Baldwin's work for the International Grenfell Association in Labrador, Canada, and his job search in New York. Most of the correspondence from the 1930s through the 1950s concerns Baldwin's work at the New York Times . There are many letters from Times owner and publisher Arthur Hays Sulzberger and others concerning the management of the New York Times , Baldwin's salary negotiations, his journalistic "scoops," and unease over what many perceived as Baldwin's blending of editorializing and factual reporting. Some correspondence between Baldwin and other Times reporters documents internal conflict over the newspaper's editorial stance on integration in the U. S. Armed Forces. Other items include a copy of an October 10, 1939 letter from Major General William C. Rivers discussing the desirability of American neutrality in World War II, and a July 7, 1950 letter from Herbert Hoover to Neil MacNeil of the New York Times praising Baldwin's work. There is also correspondence regarding Baldwin's 1955 book, Sea Fights and Shipwrecks .

Correspondence from the 1960s includes an abundance of material relating to Baldwin's work on the New York Times and Reader's Digest . It documents his often adversarial relationship with the New York Times and its staff, and his 1968 retirement from the paper. Letters and office memoranda detail growing differences between proponents and opponents of the Vietnam War on the staff and Baldwin's opinions of many of his fellow journalists, including Gay Talese, Seymour Hersch, William Beecher, and others. There are letters from Arthur Ochs ("Punch") Sulzberger, Arthur Hays Sulzberger's successor at the Times ; reporter Arthur Krock; and other staff members. In 1968 and 1969, Baldwin wrote letters to Times editors regarding what he perceived as the paper's increasingly liberal bias and one-sided coverage of the National Democratic Convention in Chicago and the Vietnam War. After his retirement, Baldwin became an editor at Reader's Digest , and there is a large amount of correspondence with Executive Editor Hobart Lewis concerning Baldwin's articles and editorials, and the Digest's commitment to furthering the conservative agenda. Additional memoranda between Baldwin and his employers at the New York Times and Reader's Digest are located in the alphabetical section of the correspondence series.

There are many letters from the 1960s between Baldwin and his editors and readers concerning his books, chiefly Battles Lost and Won and Strategy for Tomorrow , and Lippincott's Great Battles of History series. Battles Lost and Won correspondence includes an August 16, 1968 letter from V. Bogachev, First Secretary of the USSR, on the Battle of Kursk, annotated with a caustic note by Baldwin. From 1968 to 1970, while Baldwin was working on Strategy for Tomorrow , he corresponded with David Abshire and Arleigh Burke of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, as well as with private citizens and U. S. government officials, on the background and intent of the book. After its publication, he received many letters from readers expressing both praise and criticism. As editor of Lippincott's Great Battles of History series from 1960 to 1974, Baldwin corresponded with different authors. There are also letters exploring a juvenile military history series, including correspondence with Robert Silverberg, Isaac Asimov, and Barbara Tuchman.

An issue of great concern to Baldwin in the 1960s, as reflected in his correspondence, was the Vietnam War. Baldwin received several letters from Neil Sheehan on Vietnam in 1966; and from William C. ("Westy") Westmoreland, regarding his performance as commander of ground troops in Vietnam. Baldwin received many letters from readers who both criticized and praised his reporting on Vietnam and atrocities committed by American troops, including the My Lai massacre. There are also letters from his godson who served in the Marines in Vietnam, describing his life and the progress of the war from 1966 to 1968. Other issues and events of interest to Baldwin in the 1960s, which the correspondence documents, include the Cold War; the role and responsibilities of journalists reporting on the U. S. military; Robert McNamara's performance as secretary of defense; and the Yom Kippur War. There are also notes and correspondence on a 1962 incident in which Baldwin was investigated by the Kennedy administration because of leaks in the Defense Department, and numerous letters regarding Baldwin's work on a Soviet maritime and naval policy panel at the Center for Strategic Studies and his work with the Council on Foreign Relations.

Correspondence from both the 1960s and 1970s documents Baldwin's personal and professional relationships with prominent individuals in journalism, politics and government, and the U. S. armed forces, particularly the Navy, and their views on current events. Prominent correspondents from the 1960s include Grayson Kirk on October 30, 1968, on the reasons for his retirement from Columbia University; and Cyrus Vance, on his retirement from the Defense Department, June 19, 1967. Richard Allen wrote Baldwin several times in 1968 and 1969, sounding out the possibility of bringing Baldwin into the Nixon administration and asking for Baldwin's assessment of Nixon. In the 1970s, Baldwin's correspondents included Reed Irvine, H. Ross Perot, David Rockefeller, Senator Lowell Weicker, and Phyllis Schlafly.

Correspondence from the 1970s increasingly documents Baldwin's interest in and support for the conservative view on a number of topics. They include opposition to Navy commander Elmo Zumwalt's relaxation of Navy regulations for enlisted men; concern over problems stemming from integration of and racial tension in the Navy, exemplified by a June 6, 1974 letter from an African-American sailor complaining of racial discrimination on his ship; support for the B1 bomber and other defense projects; the 1976 presidential election; unhappiness with Jimmy Carter's presidency; and apprehensions about the state of the Defense Department during the Carter administration. There are also several memoranda on a 1971 controversy in the Council of Foreign Relations over the appointment of William Bundy as editor of Foreign Affairs; some members objected to his past role in developing and implementing U. S. policy towards Vietnam.

Undated correspondence includes letters from friends, readers, and staff members of the New York Times ; two short letters from General George S. Patton; and a sarcastic note from Nicholas Petrovich of the Soviet Consulate in New York, regarding Baldwin's article "The Alarming Decline of Our Military Power" in Reader's Digest .

The alphabetical correspondence section includes letters from Robert D. Ohrenschall, a geologist friend and former schoolmate of Baldwin's from Baltimore, describing his impressions of northern Rhodesia, California, and Alaska, from the 1920s to the 1970s, and several from Mary Ann Davis, an impoverished woman in Labrador describing her life, 1929-1940. Other correspondence addresses Baldwin's writings; World War II; debate over passage of the Panama Canal Treaty; and the relative virtues of Jimmy Carter and Ronald Reagan during the 1980 election. Most of the correspondence in this section is between Baldwin and his contacts and friends among historians, journalists, and military figures. Prominent correspondents include Virginius Dabney on news coverage of Vietnam; Senator Gary Hart on strategic warfare; Robert Simpson on internal politics at the New York Times ; and William Westmoreland on two histories of the Vietnam War. William Sebald wrote many letters to Baldwin about Panama, the Soviet Union, the Iran hostage crisis, CIA operations in Japan, and his concerns about both Jimmy Carter and Ronald Reagan.

There is also a wealth of material in the alphabetical section relating to Baldwin's employment by the New York Times and Reader's Digest and documenting those publications' editorial and management policies. New York Times material includes many memoranda to the newspaper's staff regarding union activities and management issues. There are also office memoranda from 1947 to 1967 on Baldwin's reporting and travel, and a memorial to Arthur Hays Sulzberger, publisher of the New York Times . Reader's Digest material consists of memoranda to the staff of the Digest on internal management and editorial policies; and many memoranda, from 1968 to 1975 from Hanson Baldwin to Reader's Digest editor Hobart Lewis, regarding possible stories, briefings on the Vietnam War, evaluations of other journalists' work, the conservative agenda at Reader's Digest , and contemporary issues such as the SALT treaty, NATO, and U. S. relations with the Soviet Union. There is related material in the chronological section of the correspondence series.

did.unittitle: Association of the United States Army
Center for Strategic and International Studies

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

did.unittitle: Smith, Katharine A. H. [Mrs. Truman Smith]
United States. Department of the Navy

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The files in this series (4 linear ft.) are divided into three sections: travel files, research files, and Strategy for Tomorrow files. Within the travel files and research files, folders are arranged alphabetically by country or topic. Within the Strategy for Tomorrow files, folders are arranged in chapter order.

Early travel files include Baldwin's notes on the people, places, and politics of France, Germany, and England, made during a trip to Europe in 1937, which include his observations on the rise of Nazism in Germany and gathering tensions in Europe. There is also press and Navy material from his visit to Bikini Atoll during the 1946 atomic tests. There is an interesting memorandum describing a trip to the Soviet Union in 1949, including Baldwin's impressions of Poland and Moscow, the political and military situation in the Soviet Union, and speculation on the country's future. Notes from a trip to Belgium in 1952 include Baldwin's observations on Belgian political organizations and NATO defenses in the region. Files from later years consist of notes, office memoranda, articles, and publications, mostly centering on U. S. political and military interests in various regions around the world and the threat to them from the Soviet Union and Communist China. The file for Iran, Turkey, and Cyprus includes Baldwin's notes from an interview he conducted with the Shah of Iran.

Baldwin's research files include notes, unclassified military documents, correspondence, briefings, and publications on Soviet military strength, maritime policy and naval strategy; the Vietnam War; the United States military and the state of United States intelligence; and morale in the United States Navy. Of particular interest are several files on the Panama Canal Treaty from 1977, which include a letter and attached folder of material from Willard Pearson of the Bi-Partisan Citizen's Committee of Americans for the Canal Treaties; correspondence and photocopies of material from Alfred J. Schweppe and others on the legality of the Panama Canal Treaty; a statement on the treaty by Baldwin; and correspondence regarding the treaty from prominent military and political figures, including Henry ("Scoop") Jackson and Barry Goldwater.

In Strategy for Tomorrow (1970), Baldwin outlined recent United States military operations. Surveying the world arena, he focused on areas where he felt conflict was likely to emerge and expounded his views on United States defense needs for the 1970s and 1980s. Research files for the book consist of Baldwin's assembled sources for each chapter, many with annotations. The files include loose notes and notebooks, office memoranda, regional strategic analyses, a few letters, and publications on Africa, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean; NATO and the 6th Fleet; the Vietnam War; the Cold War; racial tension in the United States Navy; and the United States military.

did.unittitle: Iran, Turkey, and Cyprus [including notebook of interviews with Shah and other officials]
Middle East [Israel, Saudi Arabia, Lebanon, Bahrain, and Kuwait]
Japan, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan, and Pacific Islands
Strategy, U. S. intelligence, and foreign policy

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The writings series (4 linear ft.) includes both published and unpublished articles and stories written by Hanson Baldwin while on the staffs of the Baltimore Sun , the New York Times , and Reader's Digest ; and manuscripts, notes, and other material relating to Baldwin's books, his work as an editor for J. B. Lippincott, and the many book reviews he wrote. The series is arranged in two sections: articles/stories (1.0 linear ft.) and books/book reviews (3.5 linear ft.).

The articles/stories section is arranged by publication. Within publication, the articles are arranged in alphabetical order by title. Articles written while Baldwin was working as a cub reporter for the Baltimore Sun include several lurid sketches on African-Americans in Baltimore, 1928, and a narrative of a voyage through Argentina, Uruguay, Brazil, and Central America in the 1920s.

Baldwin's early New York Times articles and fictional pieces, both published and unpublished, cover many topics, including violent crime in New York City and surrounding environs. He also served as ghost-writer for explorer G. Findlay Andrew on banditry and famine in China and wrote profiles of several prominent Americans, including businessman Howard Stix Cullman, painter Edwin H. Blashfield, Captain R. Drake White of the New York Harbor, and Detective William J. Quaine of New York.

Baldwin's early military writings for the New York Times include unpublished prewar sketches of Navy life and the United States's role in the impending war, ca. 1938. There are also impressionistic portraits and interviews by Baldwin of some of the military leaders of the world, including Zengo Yoshida, Wilhelm Keitel, Duff Cooper, Malin Craig, and William H. Leahy, and Baldwin's reflections on "the military mind."

Baldwin's later work for the New York Times and Reader's Digest , mostly written in the 1960s and 1970s, concerns military and political affairs in the United States, the Indian Ocean, the Middle East, Japan, Vietnam, and the Soviet Union. Some articles were later reworked into chapters for the book Strategy for Tomorrow .

Other material in the articles/stories section includes a paper, "Destroyer Defects," written by Baldwin in 1927 during his tenure at the United States Naval Academy, and an unpublished story on the sea.

The books/book review section, arranged alphabetically by title, chiefly consists of manuscript drafts, master copies, outlines, and notes for Hanson Baldwin's books, The Crucial Years and Strategy for Tomorrow . Other items include book reviews, publicity material, and other material relating to books by Baldwin and to his editorship of the series, Great Battles in History and exploration of a juvenile history series. There are also two folders of book reviews written by Baldwin of books on military history.

did.unittitle: Journey through South America [Argentina, Uruguay, Brazil]
Racketeering' under the Northern Lights--Economic Slavery in Labrador,
Scrapbooks [clippings of articles Baldwin wrote without a byline for the Baltimore Sun]
Miscellaneous [Baldwin's New York Times articles written without a byline]
New York in 1930--Ten Years of a Noble Experiment,
New York Times and Reader's Digest
Atrocities by United States troops in Vietnam
National Security Policy--Its Military and ParaMilitary Elements,
"We Do Not Fight Alone in Vietnam,"
Book reviews and publicity [about Baldwin writings]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The personal material series (2 linear ft.) includes Baldwin's own biographical and career files, and files on Hanson Baldwin's father, Oliver P. Baldwin, who also worked for the Baltimore Sun . The files for Hanson Baldwin include his curricula vitae and biographical sketches for press releases, interviews and articles about his writings and activities, and his speeches. Other files include clippings, memorabilia, and other items relating to Hanson Baldwin's career in journalism, civic activities, and retirement. Baldwin's F.B.L file includes correspondence between Baldwin and Robert B. Davies, who planned to write a biography of the former and who assisted Baldwin in obtaining a copy of his F.B.I. file. The files for Randall Sollenberger detail the life and death of a boyhood friend of Baldwin's who was killed in the Spanish Civil War. Oliver P. Baldwin material consists of handwritten and published articles by him, and obituaries and biographical sketches about him from 1926. In addition to correspondence, especially regarding his son Hanson Baldwin, there are letters of condolence to the family after Oliver's death.

did.unittitle: Boys Latin School, Baltimore, Maryland [correspondence and publications]
Naval Academy Alumni Association [items relating to Baldwin's work with the N.A.A.A.]
Pulitzer Prize [material from Baldwin's award in 1943 and from the 50th Anniversary of the Pulitzer Prize in 1966]
Sollenberger, Randall [boyhood friend of Baldwin's who was killed in the Spanish Civil War]
Speeches by Hanson Baldwin [published and unpublished]
"Are We Overconfident?" [University of Chicago Round Table]
"Can Germany Break Through" [Chicago Council on Foreign Relations]
"The Outlook in the Pacific" [Town Meeting of the Air]
Sea-Borne Traffic--The Battle with the Submarines [presented at the annual meeting of the Academy of Political Science]
"Should the Pacific Be Our First or Second Front?" [Town Meeting of the Air]
"The War: Two Years of History" [University of Chicago Round Table]
"Who Should Control the Atomic Bomb?" [Town Meeting of the Air]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series (1.5 linear ft.) is arranged by subject and consists of photographs from Baldwin's travels as military editor of the New York Times , particularly of his meetings with various military leaders, journalists, and politicians. There are also a few photographs from Baldwin's childhood in Baltimore and of his daughter's family.

did.unittitle: Baldwin, Hanson [with various individuals, mostly military personnel and politicians]
United States Naval Academy [Class of 1924 reunion]
